KhalilAbuibaid ChairforMachineToolsandControlSystems DepartmentofMechanicalandProcessEngineering UniversityofKaiserslautern-Landau Kaiserslautern,Germany khalil.abuibaid@rptu.de JonathanNußbaum ChairforMachineToolsandControlSystems DepartmentofMechanicalandProcessEngineering UniversityofKaiserslautern-Landau Kaiserslautern,Germany jonathan.nussbaum@rptu.de TatjanaLegler ChairforMachineToolsandControlSystems DepartmentofMechanicalandProcessEngineering UniversityofKaiserslautern-Landau Kaiserslautern,Germany tatjana.legler@rptu.de AchimWagner InnovativeFactorySystems GermanResearchCentreforArtiﬁcialIntelligence Kaiserslautern,Germany achim.wagner@dfki.de MartinRuskowski InnovativeFactorySystems GermanResearchCentreforArtiﬁcialIntelligence Kaiserslautern,Germany martin.ruskowski@dfki.de Abstract—With Moore’s law coming to its limits, the rate of increase in compute power available for processing applications is similarly coming to a halt. This implies that the compute intensive tasks, such as robotics, artiﬁcial intelligence, and high-performance space computing need innovative ways to cater their ever-increasing compute needs. One innovative way to solve com-putational bottlenecks is to bring compute and memory together, as opposed to the Von Neumann computational model, with greater degree of parallelism in an event-based, asynchronous computation paradigm. Neuromorphic computing is one such paradigm that draws its inspiration from the brain. Energy and computational efﬁciency, asynchronous and event-based process-ing being its salient features, neuromorphic computing is an area worth exploring for compute intensive tasks. In this paper, the authors explore the possibilities and beneﬁts of neuromorphic computing in robotics, and establish possible research directions that could beneﬁt the robotics domain.
Index Terms—Neuromorphic Computing, Robotics, In-memory Computation, Adaptive Control I. INTRODUCTION The design of man-made computational systems has always been inﬂuenced by the biological ones, like – the human brain.
From Turing to Von Neumann, pioneers in the areas of com-putational research have tried to model intelligent machines based on the human brain, though modern computers and biological systems (human brains) are fundamentally different systems [3].
Funded by the Carl Zeiss Stiftung In the early 1940s, computational neuroscience had a dif-ferent understanding of how computation in the brain occurs.
RODNEY BROOKS [3] refers to MCCULLOCH & PITTS [20] to give an example how the “all-or-none” model was the understanding of how neurons ﬁre in a nervous system, rightly stating that those binary abstractions of computational models do not capture the inherent complexities in the brain.
Biological computational systems have evolved to process in-formation arriving as stimuli, which are sparse, asynchronous, and event-driven in nature. Moreover, AMNON SHASHUA [3] also mentions that learning in the brain and modern computers fundamentally differ in terms of their memory and processing speed. The brain can be very parsimonious in terms of the amount of data and energy required to learn, while modern machine learning (statistical learning) requires a vast amount of random access memory capacity and data to learn from, which differs substantially from what the human brain uses.
With an energy requirement of just 20 W, the brain can do tasks like – simultaneous recognition, reasoning, planning, and control of body locomotion, while the energy requirement of standard computing techniques for just object recognition goes up to 250 W [24]. However, looking at the metric - processing capability, with 10-50 petaﬂops, the brain outperforms the state-of-the-art CPUs like – Intel Core i7s. According to JULIE GROLLIER, the energy consumption in computation primarily arises from large streams of data ﬂowing between the memory and processing units [28], which is a consequence of compu-42 2023 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Power Engineering (AIPE) 979-8-3503-1078-8/23/$31.00 ©2023 IEEE DOI 10.1109/AIPE58786.2023.00015 2023 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Power Engineering (AIPE) | 979-8-3503-1078-8/23/$31.00 ©2023 IEEE | DOI: 10.1109/AIPE58786.2023.00015 Authorized licensed use limited to: Technische Universitat Kaiserslautern. Downloaded on November 18,2024 at 10:34:28 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. tational hardwares being developed based on VON NEUMANN model. Technological advancements that bring the possibility to integrate memory and processing units together would not only bring down energy consumption for computation, but also make computation in itself faster and more efﬁcient.
Thus, even though the world of computational neuroscience has drawn inspiration from biology, it has evolved to be quite different. This brings the need to look at alternative computational paradigms.
Neuromorphic Computing is one such novel computational paradigm which inspires itself from various characteristics of the brain and bio-inspired computing like - structural and functional hierarchy through hierarchical parallelism involving successive layers with increasing receptive ﬁeld and complex-ity [3], vast connectivity and time-dependent neuronal and synaptic functionality [24]. With its bio-inspired hardware and software implementation, it beneﬁts several areas in robotics with the efﬁciencies bio-inspired systems bring. Traditional ap-proaches in artiﬁcial intelligence algorithms have seen success with computing substrates that are principally different from the algorithmic implementation. Neuromorphic computing, however, aims to organise and carry out computation in a way real nervous systems and biological brains do, going beyond VON NEUMANN computational architectures [28].
From the perspective of multi-agent systems, robots are in-herent active and autonomous agents that interact with unstruc-tured and uncontrolled real-world environments to perceive, plan, decide and execute actions [25] [30] that are similar to their biological (animals) counter-parts. Robotic use-cases are generally constrained by - power consumption, latency, adaptivity, and data efﬁciency of the algorithms used. How-ever, these constraints are in alignment with the characteristics neuromorphic computing system provide, as advantages over conventional computing systems.
In this paper, the authors present their take on how neuromorphic computing could inﬂuence industrial robotics.
They consider established problems in industrial robotics and explore how neuromorphic computing could help solve them. Since neuromorphic computing is a novel computa-tional paradigm, the authors ﬁrst explain this and establish its characteristics and relevance in section II. They then continue by brieﬂy introducing robotics and parts of a robotic system in section III, while section IV discusses how neuromorphic computing could be applied in the areas of industrial robotics.
Finally, section IV is a conclusion, summing up the paper and providing an outlook on future research on neuromorphic industrial robotics.
II. NEUROMORPHIC COMPUTING Neuromorphic Computing, ﬁrst coined by CARVER MEAD, is a quasi-digital (digital in amplitude but analogue in time) computing paradigm based on the neural computation that oc-curs similar to the biological brain, implementing perceptron-like distributed parallel information processing [21] [28].
One of the fundamental differences between a neuromorphic processing system to that of a VON NEUMANN processing system lies in the fact that, for a neuromorphic processor, both processing and memory are governed by the neurons and synapses while traditional VON NEUMANN processors have separate units for processing and memory. Programs in neuromorphic processors are deﬁned by the structure of the neural network and its parameters, differing from VON NEU-MANN processors, which do the same with explicit instructions [26]. The other fundamental difference lies in information encoding. VON NEUMANN processors encode information as numbers represented by binary values, while neuromorphic processors encode information as spikes with their associ-ated time at which they occur [26]. It, basically, adapts the fundamental natural neural characteristics of the brain like - fully integrated memory-and-computing, ﬁne-grain paral-lelism, pervasive feedback and recurrence, massive network fan-outs, low precision, stochastic computation and continuous adaptation in computational architecture [6] to strive for brain-like computational and energy efﬁciency.
A. Along these four fundamental circuit variables, there exists three fundamental circuit constants - Resistance (R), Inductance (L), and Capac-itance (C). Its electrical resistance is not constant, but dependent on the current that has previously ﬂown through it. This means a memristor remembers the most recent resistance it offered to a ﬂow of charge, even when the external power supply is turned off. This is the so-called non-volatility property [4].
To establish the analogy between the biological brain, one can say that this memristor, could be compared to that of a neural synapse. This has not only helped understand the dynamics of charged atomic or molecular species in nanoscale electronics, but also has formed the foundation for developing neuromorphic processors like – Intel Loihi [6] or SpiNNaker [9] or IBM TrueNorth [1] to name a few.
2) The Neuromorphic Algorithms: Besides the hardware, the paradigm of neuromorphic computing requires appropriate algorithms to make an asynchronous, event-based computa-tion on a neuromorphic hardware possible, thus achieving energy-efﬁciency and low-latency in computation. Like in the hardware, where memristor circuits mimic the synapses of the brain, neuromorphic algorithms at its core try to mimic the brain functionality of generating and propagating action potentials to learn and interact with the environment. The perceptron, which remains the basic element of computation, is implemented with algorithms or neuron models that better mimic the brain with spike-based sparse and temporal data processing as compared to traditional neuron and activation function used in artiﬁcial neural networks. Of the many neural models that exist, the three most important are: • Leaky Integrate and Fire (LIF) Model - The LIF model is a highly efﬁcient neural model for modelling action potential of biological neurons. It is based on the dynam-ics of current ﬂowing through an RC-Circuit, with the resistor and capacitor connected in parallel as shown in ﬁg.2.

In this model, the input current (Iin) (signals) is inte-grated over time until the membrane potential (vmem) reaches or exceeds the set threshold voltage. This simpliﬁcation also helps LIF models to be incorporated in neural networks forming the elementary part of spiking neural networks [32].

III. ROBOTICS LYNCH & PARK (2017) deﬁnes robotics as an academic discipline with the aim of creating intelligent machines that think and behave like humans [19]. Apart from the structural build, a robotic system consists of electrical and electronic systems that power, and control the system. Moreover, these robotic systems consist of sensors and instruments for per-ception, recognition, and communication. To bring these me-chanical and electrical/electronics hardware together and build a functional system, software, and algorithms are developed that enable proper integration of these part components and systems with each other. In the quest to create intelligent machines that think and behave like humans, the authors of this paper argue that neuromorphic computing that works on the principles of a biological neural system is worth an investigation for this domain. To understand the areas where an application of neuromorphic computing makes sense, this section would introduce the parts of a robotic system in general. The section following would discuss the application of neuromorphic computing, identifying areas where computation efﬁciency could lead to bottlenecks.
A. Parts / Constituents of a robotic system A robotic system consists of robot mechanisms, robotic end-effectors/actuators, sensors, control systems, and communica-tion.
1) Robot Mechanisms: A robot mechanism consists of connected rigid bodies, also known as links, connected with the help of joints, enabling relative motion between adjacent links. Robot mechanism are generally classiﬁed into open-chain arm mechanisms and closed loop mechanisms. The primary difference between these two mechanisms lies in the fact that the former has all of its joints actuated, while the latter has only a subset of the joints actuated [19].
2) Robot Actuators: Robot mechanisms (as discussed above) needs to be moved and through them force needs to be exerted to accomplish a given task. Moving these mechanisms is done by actuators. Actuators can be electric, pneumatic and hydraulic systems. Most robot actuators work by applying force or torque to the robot joints. [19].
3) Sensors: Sensors form an important component for the robot to interact with its environment. Sensors read and measure parameters of not only the external environment but also the motion of internal joints. Sensors like encoders, potentiometers, or resolvers measure the displacement and tachometers measure the velocity of joints. Similarly, force-torque sensors are used to measure the force-torque values at the robot end-effectors. Interacting with the external envi-ronment takes places with sensors like camera, radar, or lidar [19].
4) Control Systems: Control systems form a fundamental part of robotics. Based on the task at hand and the perceived environment, the robot actuators should be controlled to achieve the desired position, velocity, and acceleration. The robot control systems do this task of converting the details of tasks and environment conditions to forces and torques at the actuators. For this, several control strategies, depending on task and environment are used, like the motion control, force control, hybrid control and impedance control. Controlling a robot through its actuators requires an in-depth understanding of how forces and torques at the robot joints inﬂuence the motion of the robot. This relationship is the dynamics of robots, and it is characterised by complex equations and un-certainties. Therefore, feedback control is used to compensate for these uncertainties and complexity in the dynamic robot 46 Authorized licensed use limited to: Technische Universitat Kaiserslautern. Downloaded on November 18,2024 at 10:34:28 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. models. Control systems, thus, help in forming the basics of dynamic behaviour of the robots in areas of robot movement and interaction with the environment [19].
5) Communication: Communication is another key part of robotic systems. Communication facilitates orchestration of tasks between robots in multi-robot environments, ensuring inter-robot co-ordination to achieve common goals. Commu-nication ensures mutual exchange of information in the form of state and control, which are key for robot collaboration and co-operation [12].
IV. APPLICATION OF NEUROMORPHIC COMPUTING IN INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS This section brings together the two areas, neuromorphic computing and industrial robotics. Neuromorphic computing is a novel and potentially revolutionizing technology that imitates the complexity of the human brain. This makes it worth exploring the application of this technology in industrial robotics where besides accuracy, efﬁciency, and productivity, factors like unstructured environments, adaptability, and real-time decision-making remains as areas for further improve-ment.
A. Compute intensive areas in industrial robotics Neuromorphic computing is characterised by its novel com-putational architecture, making computation inherently parallel and energy-efﬁcient. Aiming to achieve brain-like efﬁciency, neuromorphic computing paves the path for several aspects like lifelong learning, utilisation of noisy data, and data-deﬁcient or unevenly distributed data. These characteristics of this novel computation paradigm make it specially suitable for industrial robotics or robotics in general. In contrast to the conventional robot programming by determining the behaviour of the control system based on the sensor inputs, enhanced perception and learning capability through neuromorphic com-puting could turn out to be beneﬁcial [24].
As introduced in section III, movement of robot mechanisms and control systems are areas, where computation load is substantially high and is essential to analyse for dependable robotic operations. For example, the problem of forward kinematics in robotics which deals with ﬁnding the position and orientation of the end-effectors given the joint positions or the problem of inverse kinematics which aims to ﬁnd the set of joint positions that lead to a given end-effector conﬁguration are classical examples of compute intensive areas in industrial robotics [19]. Another possible application of neuromorphic computing would be its application in adaption to a potentially dynamic gripping conditions in an industrial environment.
Though REDDY & SURESH (2013), suggest using a universal gripper that is similar to human hand, not only in terms of their sensory and control capabilities but also in terms of its anatomical conﬁguration [23], adjusting/adapting to the grip-ping conditions remains a challenge in a dynamic industrial environment. A similar research problem has been addressed by SAHU & BISWAL (2020), where the integration of sensors for measurement of parameters like – Contact Pressure, Force, Torque, Position, Surface Proﬁle in robotic hands with multiple degree of freedoms for industrial application is discussed. This leads to not only more capabilities but also more constraints.
Owing to the characteristic multiple asynchronous parallel processing streams at very low power, high throughput and low latency [25], neuromorphic computing could enhance the processing of stimuli that indicate change in the gripping conditions and thus elevate the beneﬁts of a universal gripper on the robot end-effectors in an industrial environment.
HANAI ET AL. (2022) talks about a control method, in an environment with human robot collaboration, that is capable of simultaneously adjusting operability and ampliﬁcation rate of human force for a novel control method that brings in the idea of virtual impedance, along with the effectiveness of using force feedback control and impedance control as power-assist methods. However, focus was laid on human assistance, with only a few involving robots and assist devices like the passive balancer [13]. In relation to this, it might be interesting to see how a setup with multiple collaborative robots with assist devices and humans work. The computational bottleneck, thus created, to achieve information processing speed for the coordination could be solved with the introduction of neuro-morphic computing using suitable neuromorphic hardware.
With the growing need for ﬂexible and re-conﬁgurable manufacturing systems, the trend for collaborative industrial robotics is increasing, thereby bridging the gap between fully automated and manual workstations [17]. However, safety is an important aspect to be considered by such collaborative industrial robots [18]. Especially when the possibility of humans coming into the robot’s conﬁguration space is exis-tent, dynamic sensor-based surveillance of this conﬁguration space is essential [27]. This includes human gesture, speech, and intention recognition [18]. All of these are event-based recognition and prediction tasks. Dynamic task planning and collaboration thus require a substantial non-parallel event-based computation, which makes computation in robotics so heavy. To sum up, Perception, Recognition, Dynamic and Adaptive Motion, and Communication are the essential, but compute intensive areas of realising collaborative/cooperative industrial robotics [18].

FULL CONTENT:
# Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack

###### Abstract

Robotic technologies have been an indispensable part for improving human productivity since they have been helping humans in completing diverse, complex, and intensive tasks in a fast yet accurate and efficient way.
Therefore, robotic technologies have been deployed in a wide range of applications, ranging from personal to industrial use-cases.
However, current robotic technologies and their computing paradigm still lack embodied intelligence to efficiently interact with operational environments, respond with correct/expected actions, and adapt to changes in the environments.
Toward this, recent advances in neuromorphic computing with Spiking Neural Networks (SNN) have demonstrated the potential to enable the embodied intelligence for robotics through bio-plausible computing paradigm that mimics how the biological brain works, known as “neuromorphic artificial intelligence (AI)”.
However, the field of neuromorphic AI-based robotics is still at an early stage, therefore its development and deployment for solving real-world problems expose new challenges in different design aspects, such as accuracy, adaptability, efficiency, reliability, and security.
To address these challenges, this paper will discuss how we can enable embodied neuromorphic AI for robotic systems through our perspectives:
(P1) Embodied intelligence based on effective learning rule, training mechanism, and adaptability;
(P2) Cross-layer optimizations for energy-efficient neuromorphic computing;
(P3) Representative and fair benchmarks;
(P4) Low-cost reliability and safety enhancements;
(P5) Security and privacy for neuromorphic computing; and
(P6) A synergistic development for energy-efficient and robust neuromorphic-based robotics.
Furthermore, this paper identifies research challenges and opportunities, as well as elaborates our vision for future research development toward embodied neuromorphic AI for robotics.

## I Introduction

Robotic technologies have become critical and indispensable in everyday life for improving human productivity and quality of service, because robots have capabilities to complete diverse, complex, as well as intensive tasks faster, more accurate, and more efficient as compared to humans.
Therefore, their deployments for solving real-world problems are widely spread from personal to industrial application use-cases.
However, current robotic technologies and computing paradigm still lack “intelligence”, which is defined as the capability to interact with operational environments, correctly interpret the sensors’ signals (stimulus), respond the stimulus with proper actions to accomplish the goals within the expected time frame, then learn the impact of the actions and continuously adapt to changes in uncontrolled/dynamic operational environments in an efficient manner [[1](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib1)].
Furthermore, apart from the above performance aspect (i.e., accuracy, adaptability, and efficiency), these robots are also expected to perform robust processing against reliability and security threats, thus providing reliable outputs under diverse operational environments.
These requirements can potentially be fulfilled by the neuromorphic computing paradigm through Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) [[2](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib2), [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib3), [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib1)] due to the following reasons.

SNNs employ sparse spike-based data transmission and computation, thus enabling ultra-low power/energy consumption, including both the training and inference phases [[2](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib2)][[4](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib4)].

SNNs can employ diverse learning rules, such as the bio-plausible ones, hence enabling efficient training under both supervised and unsupervised learning settings [[4](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib4)].
These (unsupervised) learning capabilities are especially beneficial to make SNN-based systems adapt to dynamic operational environments [[5](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib5)].

Recent advances in neuromorphic computing showed great success in achieving high accuracy, low latency, low memory footprint, and ultra-low power/energy consumption [[2](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib2)][[6](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib6)].

All these benefits make neuromorphic computing with SNNs is a strong candidate as the computation model and technologies for enabling intelligence in resource-constrained robotic systems under diverse operational environments.
In this paper, we refer an intelligence enabled by neuromorphic computing to as “neuromorphic artificial intelligence (AI)” or simply “neuromorphic intelligence”.

This paper delves into the intersection of neuromorphic computing and robotics, and specifically focusing on how to leverage neuromorphic AI-based approaches to achieve embodied intelligence in robotic systems.
First, this paper provides an overview of the field of neuromorphic AI-based robotics (Section [II](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S2 "II Neuromorphic AI-based Robotics ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack")), highlighting its significance and potential impact.
Then, it explores the principles and applications of neuromorphic computing with SNNs (Section [III](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S3 "III Neuromorphic Computing with SNNs ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack")), emphasizing their relevance in enabling embodied intelligence.
Finally, drawing from our insights and experiences, we offer perspectives on the development of neuromorphic AI-based robotic systems (Section [IV](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S4 "IV Our Perspectives ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack")).
This includes identifying key challenges and opportunities, and outlining our vision for future research development aimed at advancing the realization of embodied neuromorphic AI for robotics.

## II Neuromorphic AI-based Robotics

### II-A System Overview

Neuromorphic AI-based robotics integrates neuromorphic computing with SNNs to realize artificial intelligence [[6](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib6)].
These robots need to autonomously interact with the environments [[1](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib1)], thereby necessitating efficient integration of the sensors, computation, and actuation modules, as shown in Fig. [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S2.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ II-A System Overview ‣ II Neuromorphic AI-based Robotics ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack").

![Refer to caption]()

#### II-A1 Sensors Subsystem

This subsystem plays a pivotal role in the neuromorphic AI-based robotics for facilitating efficient interaction with the operational environments.
These sensors encompass a wide range of input devices aimed at capturing diverse environmental data essential for the robots’ autonomous operations.
Event-based sensors offer rapid data acquisition capabilities, thereby enabling the system to react swiftly to dynamic surroundings [[7](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib7)].
Meanwhile, the conventional sensors such as Global Positioning System (GPS), Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR), Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) and ultrasonic, also contribute to precise robot positioning and navigation, which are crucial for navigating complex environments with high accuracy.
Furthermore, environmental sensors such as thermal and gas detectors, provide valuable insights into the surroundings, ensuring the robots’ adaptability to various conditions [[1](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib1)].
Incorporating sophisticated data fusion techniques enhances the systems’ ability to interpret and utilize complex environmental information effectively [[8](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib8)].
Furthermore, embracing multi-modality sensing and computing not only enriches the robots’ perception capabilities but also enhances its overall robustness and adaptability [[1](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib1)][[8](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib8)].

#### II-A2 Computation Subsystem

This subsystem is responsible for performing the main SNN computation, which encompasses cross-layer design aspects: software (SW) part and hardware (HW) part.

Software:
If the input samples come in the form of events/spikes (e.g., from DVS camera), then spike/neural encoding is performed to provide interpretable sequences of spikes (i.e., spike trains).
Meanwhile, if the input samples come in the form of conventional analog or digital values, then data conversion to spikes is required.
Such a conversion is typically coupled with neural encoding.
For instance, a higher intensity image pixel is converted using a rate coding into a spike train with a higher spiking rate than the lower intensity one [[9](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib9)].
Furthermore, we may need a pre-processing like denoising or filtering to ensure that the input samples are ideal for conversion and further processing [[10](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib10)].
The generated spike trains are then fed to the network model for main SNN processing.
This processing is mapped and scheduled to run on the targeted hardware platform (e.g., neuromorphic HW accelerator) while considering the application and hardware constraints.
Outputs of this processing are interpreted based on the neural coding to derive an action or decision for the robot actuation.
This processing is usually for inference, but can also be used for online training if the robotic system is designed to update its knowledge at run time for adapting to changes in the operational environments (i.e., continual learning) [[11](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib11)].
Therefore, feedback from the actuation subsystem can be utilized for learning the impact of the previous action, and hence improving the adaptability.

Hardware:
To efficiently expedite SNN processing, the robotic system needs to employ neuromorphic-based platform as the underlying hardware, since spike-based operations in SNNs are different from conventional Artificial Neural Network (ANN) operations [[12](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib12)].
Hence, to meet this requirement, researchers in the neuromorphic community have proposed different neuromorphic accelerators/processors [[12](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib12)].
Furthermore, SNN processing should also be mapped and scheduled judiciously considering the dataflow of underlying hardware architecture to maximize the latency and energy efficiency benefits.

#### II-A3 Actuation Subsystem

This subsystem has a pivotal role for realizing action (actuation) based on the given command.
Therefore, application constraints should be communicated to the computation part, so that the generated response meets the requirements (e.g., maximum response time).
Furthermore, to improve the adaptability of the robotic system, the impact of the previous action should be used as feedback for enriching and updating the robots’ knowledge through a continual learning mechanism.

SNN deployment in robotics encompasses various applications, including path planning, simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM), and adaptive control, among others.
Path planning applications are addressed through diverse approaches such as feed-forward SNNs with Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity (STDP) for drone navigation [[8](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib8)], LSTM-SNNs with statistical learning for drone recognition [[13](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib13)], and SNNs with STDP for SLAM [[15](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib15), [16](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib16)].
Control applications utilize feed-forward SNNs with reinforcement learning (RL) for object detection and obstacle avoidance [[17](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib17)], SNNs with threshold plasticity for adaptive tracking control [[18](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib18)], and SNNs with reward-modulated STDP and RL for lane-keeping navigation [[20](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib20)].
This structured framework, summarized in Table [I](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S2.T1 "Table I ‣ II-B Current Trends of Neuromorphic-based Robotic Systems ‣ II Neuromorphic AI-based Robotics ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack"), aids in selecting suitable SNN methods for specific robotic tasks, considering factors like network complexity and HW constraints, thus advancing robotics applications.

While significant progress has been made in the field of neuromorphic AI-based robotics, it remains at an early stage of development.
Current state-of-the-art works primarily concentrate on enhancing learning quality (e.g., accuracy), but benchmarks are still limited.
Moreover, little attention has been given to exploring reliability and security aspects to ensure robust processing in SNNs. Consequently, the absence of an end-to-end framework for developing energy-efficient and robust neuromorphic-based robots persists as a challenge.

### II-C Open Challenges

Based on the lessons learned from the current trends of the neuromorphic-based robotic systems, we identify multiple open challenges to be addressed for pushing the field forward and giving immediate and long-term impact in academics and industry.
These challenges encompass a wide range of aspects, as the following.

Intelligence for Robotics:
This aspect demands robotic systems to have capabilities for accomplishing the goals (e.g., accuracy) and adapting to changes in the operational environments (i.e., adaptability).

Energy-Efficient Processing:
This aspect demands energy efficiency for SNN processing in the robotic system.
Therefore, it also demands low processing power and latency.

Limited Benchmarks for System Developments:
Currently, we have limited benchmarks for supporting the developments of neuromorphic-based robotic systems, thereby limiting the quick progress of the field.

Reliability and Safety:
This aspect demands reliable processing in robotic systems in the presence of reliability and safety threats, thereby ensuring the correctness and fidelity of the generated output/decision.

Security and Privacy:
This aspect demands secure processing and privacy-preserving mechanism in robotic systems in the presence of security and privacy threats, thereby securing important and confidential information.

Limited Supports in Neuromorphic-based Robotic Developments:
Currently, there are limited supports (e.g., frameworks and tools) in developing robust and energy-efficient neuromorphic computing for robotics, hence limiting the adoption and deployment of neuromorphic-based robotic systems.

These challenges and the corresponding opportunities are discussed further in Section [IV](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S4 "IV Our Perspectives ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack") while elaborating our perspectives and visions.

## III Neuromorphic Computing with SNNs

SNNs offer high plausibility to the biological brain, since they employ spikes for transmitting information and spiking neurons for processing information.
SNNs also have capabilities to learn spatial and temporal (spatio-temporal) information from the input samples.
In SNN processing, input samples are converted into input spike trains using a neural coding.
These input spikes are then fed to the SNN, which has a specific network architecture (topology).
In each neuron, the input spikes will increase the neurons’ membrane potential, and if the potential reaches the defined threshold, then the respective neuron generates output spikes.
Details of the neuronal dynamics may differ across different neuron models.
Afterward, the output spikes are decoded to interpret the information.
In the training phase, a specific learning rule is employed for extracting information from input features and updating the weights (i.e., knowledge) of the SNN model; while in the inference phase, the model infers input samples based on its learned knowledge.
In summary, an SNN model consists of several components: network architecture, neuron model, spike/neural coding, and learning rule [[4](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib4)] (see an overview of the SNN processing pipeline in Fig. [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S3.F3 "Figure 3 ‣ III Neuromorphic Computing with SNNs ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack")), which are briefly discussed in the following.

![Refer to caption]()

Network Architecture:
It defines how neurons are connected to each others via synapses.
There are several connection types, such as convolution, full-connected (dense), recurrent, or even combination from multiple architecture types [[6](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib6)].

Neuron Model:
Several neuron models have been proposed in the literature, e.g., Hodgkin-Huxley, Integrate-and-Fire (IF), Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF), and Izhikevich.
LIF neuron model is employed widely in the neuromorphic community as it can provide diverse spike representations like brain signals with a relatively low computational complexity [[4](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib4)]; see LIF definition in Eq. [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S3.E1 "Equation 1 ‣ 2nd item ‣ III Neuromorphic Computing with SNNs ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack").

Spike/Neural Coding:
It defines how information is represented in spikes (i.e., encoding), and how spikes are interpreted (i.e., decoding).
Several coding schemes have been proposed in the literature, such as rate (frequency), burst, rank order, and time-to-first spike [[6](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib6)].

Learning Rule:
It defines how the learning mechanism is performed during the training process.
Several learning rules have been proposed in the literature, and they employ different approaches which can be classified as the following.

ANN-to-SNN Conversion:
This approach performs training in non-spiking (ANN) domain using back-propagation techniques [[23](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib23)].
Once the training phase is finished, the trained model is converted into an SNN model.
Therefore, this approach is typically performed in supervised learning settings.

Direct SNN Training:
This approach performs training directly in spiking domain.
Currently, there are two prominent techniques: (1) approximation-based back-propagation known as surrogate gradient learning [[24](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib24)]; and (2) layer-wise local learning [[25](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib25)].
This approach is performed under supervised learning settings to reduce the loss function, hence achieving state-of-the-art accuracy for SNNs.

Bio-Plausible Learning:
This approach also performs training directly in spiking domain using techniques inspired by biological phenomena in the brains, like Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity (STDP) learning rule [[4](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib4)].
These rules perform learning locally in each synapse without having to know the labels, hence supporting unsupervised learning settings.
In this manner, these rules enable efficient unsupervised continual learning where the robotics gather (unlabeled) data directly from the environments, learn from them, and update the knowledge accordingly [[5](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib5)] [[11](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib11)].

## IV Our Perspectives

We now present our perspectives regarding new challenges and opportunities introduced by the neuromorphic AI-based robotics in several aspects which are correlated closely, as the following.

Embodied Neuromorphic Intelligence for Robotics:
“Enabling neuromorphic intelligence in robotic systems requires novel design methods beyond the traditional ones which rely on the offline training and the SNN model deployment without any knowledge updates”.

HW/SW-level Optimizations for Energy-Efficient Neuromorphic Computing:
“Maximizing energy efficiency of the neuromorphic computing in robotics needs to consider cross-layer optimizations covering both the HW and SW layers that complement each other”.

Representative and Fair Benchmarks for Robotics:
“It is required to provide representative and fair benchmarks for robotic developments that cover different sensory functions and objective tasks under diverse environment settings”.

Low-Cost Reliability and Safety Enhancements:
“Low-cost techniques are required to enhance the reliability and safety of the neuromorphic-based robotic systems, i.e., reliability against device faults to ensure safety of robots’ behavior”.

Security and Privacy for Neuromorphic Computing:
“Cost-effective techniques are needed to ensure the security and privacy of the neuromorphic-based robotic systems, i.e., security against attacks and privacy-preserving mechanism”.

A Synergistic Development for Energy-Efficient and Robust Neuromorphic-based Robotic Systems:
“It is important to have an end-to-end development framework and tools for the full processing pipeline of a robotic system (i.e., sensors, computation, and actuation) across the HW and SW layers”.

In the following sub-sections, we elaborate these perspectives in more depth while providing some early evidences supporting our visions on how to move forward with the opportunities.

### IV-A P1: Embodied Neuromorphic Intelligence for Robotics

Realizing the neuromorphic intelligence in robots needs the capabilities to accomplish the targeted learning quality (e.g., accuracy or precision) and the adaptability to interact with the environments.

Learning Quality:
To accomplish the targeted learning quality, a robot requires to perform appropriate actions by relying on its perception and existing knowledge.
The targeted quality typically depends on the application use-case, because different use-cases often require (1) different data types from sensors for perception, (2) different objective tasks to solve through appropriate training mechanism and learning rule, and (3) different expected actions.
Therefore, in our perspective, it is important to design a specialized SNN processing pipeline for each application use-case to achieve the targeted learning quality through a synergistic design and/or selection of network architecture, learning rule, and neural coding.
Center of this idea is to develop high quality learning rule and training mechanism that maximally learn the spatio-temporal information from the input samples.
Here, multi-modality sensing will enrich the input information for guiding the training process better.

Adaptability:
The application use-case also defines the expected level of adaptability of the robot.
For instance, a robot that operates in a controlled environment and execute repetitive actions (e.g., an industrial assembly robot) requires a lower adaptability level as compared to the one that has to continuously infer and adapt to dynamically-changing operational environments, including changes in the robotic platform itself (e.g., battery and thermal status).
Such adaptive capabilities are important due to the following reasons.

Some application use-cases are physically too far away and/or dangerous for humans to explore (e.g., space, active volcano, deep forest, and open sea), thereby requiring smart autonomous agents/robots that can adapt to the unseen conditions [[26](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib26)].

The knowledge learned from the offline training may become obsolete over time, which leads to low accuracy at run time in dynamic environments; see 1 in Fig. [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S4.F4 "Figure 4 ‣ IV-A P1: Embodied Neuromorphic Intelligence for Robotics ‣ IV Our Perspectives ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack")(a).

Dynamic environments often have unseen data that need to be learned online.
Hence, the robot requires knowledge updates through online training (learning) by leveraging unlabeled data that are gathered directly from the environments [[5](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib5)].

Robots also need to trade-off their performance (e.g., accuracy and latency) with processing power/energy for saving battery.

Recent progress has demonstrated the SNN potentials for overcoming unsupervised continual learning challenges.
For instance, recent works [[11](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib11)][[5](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib5)] improve the accuracy of baseline network (with 32-bit weights and STDP rule) using learning rule enhancements; see 2 in Fig. [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#S4.F4 "Figure 4 ‣ IV-A P1: Embodied Neuromorphic Intelligence for Robotics ‣ IV Our Perspectives ‣ Embodied Neuromorphic Artificial Intelligence for Robotics: Perspectives, Challenges, and Research Development Stack")(b).
The work of [[11](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib11)] enables unsupervised continual learning by leveraging spiking activities to enrich the STDP learning process [[11](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib11)].
Then, this concept is further extended in [[5](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib5)] by employing a guided fine-tuning on different learning parameters to enable network with lower weight precision to achieve comparable accuracy to the ones with higher weight precision [[5](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib5)].
However, the state-of-the-art works still consider the MNIST dataset for evaluating their accuracy [[5](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03325v1#bib.bib5)].
Therefore, in our perspective, it is important to develop high quality learning rules and training mechanism that can overcome diverse environments, by leveraging unlabeled data gathered during run time for updating the knowledge.

![Refer to caption]()

Research developments and opportunities:
To summarize, we list new opportunities that arise from from the requirements of embodied neuromorphic intelligence for robotics.

A specialized SNN processing pipeline should be developed for each application use-case to achieve high learning quality with advanced SNN architectures and learning techniques.

## Abstract

The design of robots that interact autonomously with the environment and exhibit complex behaviours is an open challenge that can benefit from understanding what makes living beings fit to act in the world. Neuromorphic engineering studies neural computational principles to develop technologies that can provide a computing substrate for building compact and low-power processing systems. We discuss why endowing robots with neuromorphic technologies – from perception to motor control – represents a promising approach for the creation of robots which can seamlessly integrate in society. We present initial attempts in this direction, highlight open challenges, and propose actions required to overcome current limitations.

## Opportunities and challenges

Neuromorphic circuits and sensorimotor architectures represent a key enabling technology for the development of a unique generation of autonomous agents endowed with embodied neuromorphic intelligence. We define intelligence as the ability to efficiently interact with the environment, to plan adequate behaviour based on the correct interpretation of sensory signals and internal states, for accomplishing its goals, to learn and predict the effects of its actions, and to continuously adapt to changes in unconstrained scenarios. Ultimately, embodied intelligence allows the robot to interact swiftly with the environment in a wide range of conditions and tasks[1](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR1 "Barrett, L. Beyond the Brain: How Body and Environment Shape Animal 5and Human Minds (Princeton University Press, 2011). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1515/9781400838349
                  
                . Barrett provides an in-depth overview on what shapes human and animal’s intelligent behaviour, exploiting their brains, but also bodies and environment. She describes how physical structure contributes to cognition, and how it employs materials and resources in specific environments."). Doing this “efficiently” means performing robust processing of information with minimal use of resources such as power, memory and area, while coping with noise, variability, and uncertainty. These requirements entail finding solutions which improve performance and increase robustness in a way that is different from the standard engineering approach of adding general purpose computing resources, redundancy, and control structures in the system.

Current progress in both machine learning and computational neuroscience is producing impressive results in Artificial Intelligence (AI)[2](#ref-CR2 "LeCun, Y., Bengio, Y. & Hinton, G. Deep learning. Nature 521, 436–444 (2015)."),[3](#ref-CR3 "Schmidhuber, J. Deep learning in neural networks: an overview. Neural Netw. 61, 85–117 (2015)."),[4](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR4 "Sejnowski, T. J. The unreasonable effectiveness of deep learning in artificial intelligence. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. (2020). 
                  https://www.pnas.org/content/early/2020/01/23/1907373117.full.pdf
                  
                ."). However, conventional computing and robotic technologies are still far from performing as well as humans or other animals in tasks that require embodied intelligence[1](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR1 "Barrett, L. Beyond the Brain: How Body and Environment Shape Animal 5and Human Minds (Princeton University Press, 2011). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1515/9781400838349
                  
                . Barrett provides an in-depth overview on what shapes human and animal’s intelligent behaviour, exploiting their brains, but also bodies and environment. She describes how physical structure contributes to cognition, and how it employs materials and resources in specific environments."),[5](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR5 "Jordan, M. I. Artificial intelligence—the revolution hasn’t happened yet. Harvard Data Sci. Rev. 1 (2019-07-01). 
                  https://hdsr.mitpress.mit.edu/pub/wot7mkc1
                  
                ."). Examples are spatial perception tasks for making long-term navigation plans, coupled with fine motor control tasks that require fast reaction times, and adaptation to external conditions. Within this context, a core requirement for producing intelligent behaviour is the need to process data on multiple timescales. This multi-scale approach is needed to support immediate perception analysis, hierarchical information extraction and memorisation of temporally structured data for life-long learning, adaptation and memory reorganisation. While conventional computing can implement processes on different timescales by means of high-precision (e.g. 32-bit floating point) numerical parameters and long-term storage of data in external memory banks, this results in power consumption figures and area/volume requirements of the corresponding computational substrate that are vastly worse than those of biological neural networks[6](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR6 "Silver, D. et al. Mastering the game of go with deep neural networks and tree search. Nature 529, 484–489 (2016).").

The neuromorphic engineering approach employs mixed-signal analogue/digital hardware that supports the implementation of neural computational primitives inspired by biological intelligence that are radically different from those used in classical von Neumann architectures[7](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR7 "Indiveri, G. & Liu, S.-C. Memory and information processing in neuromorphic systems. Proc. IEEE 103, 1379–1397 (2015)."). This approach provides energy-efficient and compact solutions that can support the implementation of intelligence and its embodiment on robotic platforms[8](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR8 "Indiveri, G. & Sandamirskaya, Y. The importance of space and time for signal processing in neuromorphic agents. IEEE Signal Process. Mag. 36, 16–28 (2019)."). However, adopting this approach in robotics requires overcoming several barriers that often discourage the research community from following this promising avenue. The challenges range from the system integration of full-custom neuromorphic chips with sensors, conventional computing modules and motors, to the “programming” of the neural processing systems integrated on neuromorphic chips, up to the need for a principled framework for implementing and combining computational primitives, functions and operations in these devices using neural instead of digital representations.

Both conventional and neuromorphic robotics face the challenge of developing robust and adaptive modules to solve a wide range of tasks especially in applications in human-robot collaboration settings. Both will benefit from a framework designed to combine such modules to deliver a truly autonomous artificial agent. In this perspective, we discuss the current challenges of robotics and neuromorphic technology, and suggest possible research directions for overcoming current roadblocks and enabling the construction of intelligent robotic systems of the future, powered by neuromorphic technology.

## Requirements for intelligent robots

Recent developments in machine learning, supported by increasingly powerful and accessible computational resources, led to impressive results in robotics-specific applications[2](#ref-CR2 "LeCun, Y., Bengio, Y. & Hinton, G. Deep learning. Nature 521, 436–444 (2015)."),[3](#ref-CR3 "Schmidhuber, J. Deep learning in neural networks: an overview. Neural Netw. 61, 85–117 (2015)."),[4](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR4 "Sejnowski, T. J. The unreasonable effectiveness of deep learning in artificial intelligence. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. (2020). 
                  https://www.pnas.org/content/early/2020/01/23/1907373117.full.pdf
                  
                ."). Nevertheless, except for the case of precisely calibrated robots performing repetitive operations in controlled environments, autonomous operations in natural settings are still challenging due to the variability and unpredictability of the dynamic environments in which they act.

The interaction with uncontrolled environments and human collaborators requires the ability to continuously infer, predict and adapt to the state of the environment, of humans, and of the robotic platform itself, as described in Box [1](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#Sec3). Current machine learning, deep networks, and AI methods for robotics are not best suited for these types of scenarios and their use still has critical roadblocks that hinder their full exploitation. These methods typically require high computational (and power) resources: for example deep networks have a very large number of parameters, they need to be trained with very large datasets, and require a large amount of training time, even when using large Graphics Processing Unit (GPU) clusters. The datasets used are mostly disembodied, while ideally, for robotic applications, they would need to be tailored[9](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR9 "Pasquale, G., Ciliberto, C., Odone, F., Rosasco, L. & Natale, L. Are we done with object recognition? the icub robot’s perspective. Robot. Autonomous Syst. 112, 260–281 (2019).") and platform specific. This is especially true for end-to-end reinforcement learning, where the dataset depends on the robot plant and actuation. Data acquisition and dataset creation are expensive and time consuming. While virtual simulations can partially improve this aspect, transfer learning techniques do not always solve the problem of adapting pre-trained architectures to real-world applications. Off-line training on large datasets with thousands of parameters also implies the use of high performance, powerful but expensive and power-hungry computing infrastructures. Inference suffers less from this problem and can be run on less demanding, embedded platforms, but at the cost of very limited or no adaptation abilities, thus making the system brittle to real-world, ever-changing scenarios[10](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR10 "Hadsell, R., Rao, D., Rusu, A. & Pascanu, R. Embracing change: continual learning in deep neural networks. Trends Cogn. Sci. 24, 1028–1040 (2020).").

The key requirements in robotics are hence to reduce or possibly eliminate the need for data- and computation-hungry algorithms, making efficient use of sensory data, and to develop solutions for continuous online learning where robots can acquire new knowledge by means of weak- or self-supervision. An important step toward this goal is moving from static (or frame-based) to dynamic (or event-based) computing paradigms, able to generalise and adapt to different application scenarios, users, robots, and goals.

Neuromorphic perception addresses these problems right from the sensory acquisition level. It uses novel bio-inspired sensors that efficiently encode sensory signals with asynchronous event-based strategies[11](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR11 "Liu, S.-C. & Delbruck, T. Neuromorphic sensory systems. Curr. Opin. Neurobiol. 20, 288–295 (2010)."). It also adopts computational primitives that extract information from the events obtained from the sensors, relying on a diverse set of spike-driven computing modules.

Neuromorphic behaviour follows control policies that adapt to different environmental and operating conditions by integrating multiple sensory inputs, using event-based computational primitives to accomplish a desired task.

Both neuromorphic perception and behaviour are based on computational primitives that are derived from models of neural circuits in biological brains and that are therefore very well suited for being implemented using mixed signal analogue/digital circuits[12](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR12 "Chicca, E., Stefanini, F., Bartolozzi, C. & Indiveri, G. Neuromorphic electronic circuits for building autonomous cognitive systems. Proc. IEEE 102(September), 1367–1388 (2014). A description of neuromorphic computational primitives, their implementation in mixed-mode subthreshold CMOS circuits, and their computational relevance in supporting cognitive functions."). This offers an efficient technological substrate for neuromorphic perception and actions in robotics. Examples are context-dependent cooperative and competitive information processing, and learning and adaptation at multiple temporal scales[13](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR13 "Qiao, N. et al. A reconfigurable on-line learning spiking neuromorphic processor comprising 256 neurons and 128k synapses. Front. Neurosci. 9, 141 (2015)."),[14](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR14 "Qiao, N., Bartolozzi, C. & Indiveri, G. An ultralow leakage synaptic scaling homeostatic plasticity circuit with configurable time scales up to 100 ks. IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Circuits and Systems 11, 1271–1277 (2017).").

The development and integration of neuromorphic perception and behaviour using hardware neuromorphic computational primitives has the final goal of designing a robot with end-to-end neuromorphic intelligence as shown in Fig. [1](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#Fig1).

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-28487-2/MediaObjects/41467_2022_28487_Fig1_HTML.png)

Some non exhaustive examples of perception (magenta), intelligent behaviour (green) up to action execution (blue) that would all be implemented by means of dedicated Spiking Neural Network (SNN) hardware technology. iCub picture ©IIT author Agnese Abrusci.

In the next sections, we present an overview of the neuromorphic perception, action planning, and cognitive processing strategies, highlighting features and problems of the current state of the art in these domains. We conclude with a road map and a “call for action” to make progress in the field of embodied neuromorphic intelligence.

### Box 1 The need for adaptation in robotics

While the majority of industrial robots are currently operating in controlled settings to execute programmable and repetitive actions, robotics research is moving towards human-robot collaboration scenarios, where robots are expected to interact and collaborate with humans in uncontrolled environments in daily tasks[133](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR133 "Ajoudani, A. et al. Progress and prospects of the human–robot collaboration. Autonomous Robots 42, 957–975 (2018)."),[134](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR134 "Siva, S. & Zhang, H. Robot perceptual adaptation to environment changes for long-term human teammate following. The International Journal of Robotics Research 0278364919896625."). Different individuals’ behavioural and environmental physical conditions might change across days and tasks. The ability of robots to adapt is hence crucial for functioning in the real world and interacting with humans[135](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR135 "Tirupachuri, Y. et al. Towards partner-aware humanoid robot control under physical interactions. In (eds Bi, Y., Bhatia, R. & Kapoor, S.) Intelligent Systems and Applications, 1073–1092 (Springer International Publishing, 2020). Example paper on the complexity of the physical interaction of robots and humans, i.e. two highly dynamical systems that need to cooperate to achieve a common goal in unconstrained scenarios."). In the majority of applications, including in industry, the robot plant wears out over time, and the controller needs to adapt to changes on the plant characteristics over very long time scales. In rehabilitation robotics, the controller needs to adapt to the progress of each individual during therapy as well as to different patients, requiring adaptation both over long and short temporal scales[136](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR136 "Udupa, S., Kamat, V. R. & Menassa, C. C. Shared autonomy in assistive mobile robots: a review. Disability and Rehabilitation: Assistive Technology 1–22 (2021). Review of the progress in the field of assistive mobile robotics that highlights the need for adaptation to the user intentions (to give full control to the user) and to the varying environment (for safety)."). In most interactive applications robots must also be able to react to sudden environmental changes over short time scales, for example by switching to previously learned configurations. Unmanned autonomous robotic vehicles need to cope with changes in the environment, such as wind strength and direction; humanoid and roving robots need to adapt to different types of terrains[137](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR137 "Magaña, O. A. V. et al. Fast and continuous foothold adaptation for dynamic locomotion through cnns. IEEE Robot. Autom. Lett. 4, 2140–2147 (2019)."); artificial hands need to learn to manipulate objects of different weight and softness.

Biology provides a rich set of examples to address these needs, adapting to the changes described above[138](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR138 "Gjorgjieva, J., Drion, G. & Marder, E. Computational implications of biophysical diversity and multiple timescales in neurons and synapses for circuit performance. Curr. Opin. Neurobiol. 37, 44–52 (2016)."),[139](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR139 "Marom, S. Neural timescales or lack thereof. Prog. Neurobiol. 90, 16–28 (2010)."). On short time scales, biological systems can adapt away constant inputs with short-term plasticity mechanisms[140](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR140 "Abbott, L., Sen, K., Varela, J. & Nelson, S. Synaptic depression and cortical gain control. Science 275, 220–223 (1997)."); for longer time scales, their sensors can adapt their sensitivity to the level of the encoded signal (e.g., photoreceptors adapt to the global illumination level, to become more sensitive in dim illumination, or less sensitive under direct sun light)[141](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR141 "Shapley, R. & Enroth-Cugell, C. Chapter 9 visual adaptation and retinal gain controls. Prog. Retinal Res. 3, 263–346 (1984)."). On very long time scales, homoeostatic mechanisms regulate the overall neural activity to keep it within defined bounds, thus coping with slow changes in the environment, or in the population’s overall drive[142](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR142 "Turrigiano, G., Leslie, K., Desai, N., Rutherford, L. & Nelson, S. Activity-dependent scaling of quantal amplitude in neocortical neurons. Nature 391, 892–896 (1998).").

### Neuromorphic perception

Robots typically include many sensors that gather information about the external world, such as cameras, microphones, pressure sensors (for touch), lidars, time-of-flight sensors, temperature sensors, force-torque sensor,s or proximity sensors. In conventional setups, all sensors measure their corresponding physical signal and sample it at fixed temporal intervals, irrespective of the state and dynamics of the signal itself. They typically provide a series of static snapshots of the external world. When the signal is static, they keep on transmitting redundant data, but with no additional information, and can miss important samples when the signal changes rapidly, with a trade-off between sampling rate (for capturing dynamic signals) and data load. Conversely, in most neuromorphic sensory systems, the sensed signal is sampled and converted into digital pulses (or “events”, or “spikes”) only when there is a large enough change in the signal itself, using event-based time encoding schemes[15](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR15 "Lazar, A. A. & Tóth, L. T. Perfect recovery and sensitivity analysis of time encoded bandlimited signals. IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers. 51, 2060–2073 (2004)."),[16](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR16 "Karen, A., Scholefield, A., & Vetterli M. Sampling and reconstruction of bandlimited signals with multi-channel time encoding. IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing 68, 1105–1119 (2020).") such as pulse-density or sigma-delta modulation[17](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR17 "Singh Alvarado, A., Rastogi, M., Harris, J. G. & Príncipe, J. C. The integrate-and-fire sampler: a special type of asynchronous σ-δ modulator. In 2011 IEEE International Symposium of Circuits and Systems (ISCAS), 2031–2034 (2011)."). The data acquisition is hence adapted to the signal dynamics, with the event rate increasing for rapidly changing stimuli and decreasing for slowly changing ones. This type of encoding does not lose information[18](#ref-CR18 "Akolkar, H. et al. What can neuromorphic event-driven precise timing add to spike-based pattern recognition? Neural Comput. 27, 561–593 (2015)."),[19](#ref-CR19 "Bartolozzi, C. et al. Event-driven encoding of off-the-shelf tactile sensors for compression and latency optimisation for robotic skin. In 2017 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS), 166–173 (2017-09)."),[20](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR20 "Scheerlinck, C. et al. Fast image reconstruction with an event camera. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV) (2020-03).") and is extremely effective in scenarios with sparse activity. This event-representation is key for efficient, fast, robust and highly-informative sensing. The technological improvement comprises a reduced need for data transmission, storage and processing, coupled with high temporal resolution – when needed – and low latency. This is extremely useful for real time robotic applications.

Starting from the design of motion sensors and transient imagers[21](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR21 "Kramer, J. An integrated optical transient sensor. IEEE Trans. Circuits Syst. II: Analog Digital Signal Process. 49, 612–628 (2002)."), the first event-driven vision sensors with enough resolution, low noise and sensor mismatch – the Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS)[22](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR22 "Lichtsteiner, P., Posch, C. & Delbruck, T. A 128x128 120 dB 15 μs latency asynchronous temporal contrast vision sensor. IEEE J. Solid-State Circuits 43, 566–576 (2008). This paper describes the first event-driven sensor used outside the designer’s lab. The DVS usability (robust hardware and friendly open source software) pushed the field of neuromorphic vision.") and Asynchronous Temporal Imaging Sensor (ATIS)[23](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR23 "Posch, C., Matolin, D. & Wohlgenannt, R. A QVGA 143 dB dynamic range frame-free PWM image sensor with lossless pixel-level video compression and time-domain CDS. IEEE J. Solid-State Circuits 46, 259–275 (2011).") – triggered the development of diverse algorithms for event-driven visual processing and their integration on robotic platforms[24](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR24 "Gallego, G. et al. Event-based vision: a survey. IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence 44, 154–180 (2020). Comprehensive review of the plethora of different approaches used i event-driven vision, from adapting computer vision and DL, to biologically inspired vision."). These sensor information encoding methods break decades of static frame encoding as used by conventional cameras. Their novelty calls for the development of a new principled approach to event-driven perception. The event-driven implementation of machine vision approaches vastly outperforms conventional algorithmic solutions in specific tasks such as fast object tracking[25](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR25 "Glover, A., Vasco, V. & Bartolozzi, C. A controlled-delay event camera framework for on-line robotics. In 2018 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (2018-05)."), optical flow[26](#ref-CR26 "Benosman, R., Ieng, S.-H., Clercq, C., Bartolozzi, C. & Srinivasan, M. Asynchronous frameless event-based optical flow. Neural Netw. 27, 32–37 (2012)."),[27](#ref-CR27 "Gallego, G., Rebecq, H. & Scaramuzza, D. A unifying contrast maximization framework for event cameras, with applications to motion, depth, and optical flow estimation. In IEEE Int. Conf. Comput. Vis. Pattern Recog.(CVPR), vol. 1 (2018)."),[28](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR28 "Zhu, A. Z., Yuan, L., Chaney, K. & Daniilidis, K. Unsupervised event-based learning of optical flow, depth, and egomotion. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) (2019-06).") or stereo[29](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR29 "Zhou, Y., Gallego, G. & Shen, S. Event-based stereo visual odometry. IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37, 1–18 (2021).") and Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping (SLAM)[30](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR30 "Vidal, A. R., Rebecq, H., Horstschaefer, T. & Scaramuzza, D. Ultimate SLAM? combining events, images, and imu for robust visual SLAM in hdr and high-speed scenarios. IEEE Robot. Autom. Lett. 3, 994–1001 (2018)."). However, these algorithms and their hardware implementations still suffer from task specificity and limited adaptability.

These event-driven sensory-processing modules will progressively substitute their frame-based counterparts in robotic pipelines (see Fig. [2](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#Fig2)). However, despite the promising results, the uptake of event-driven sensing in robotics is still difficult due to the mindset change that is required to work with streams of events, instead of static frames. Furthermore, this new data representation calls for the development of new ad hoc interfaces, communication protocols (described in Box [2](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#Sec5) and Fig. [3](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#Fig3)) and software libraries for handling events. Open source JAVA[31](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR31 "Delbruck, T. Jaer open source project. 
                  http://jaerproject.org
                  
                 (2007).") and C++[32](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR32 "Glover, A., Vasco, V., Iacono, M. & Bartolozzi, C. The event-driven software library for yarp with algorithms and icub applications. Front. Robot. AI. 4, 73 (2017)."),[33](/articles/s41467-022-28487-2#ref-CR33 "Mueggler, E., Huber, B. & Scaramuzza, D. Event-based, 6-DOF pose tracking for high-speed maneuvers. In Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS), 2014 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on, 2761–2768 (IEEE, 2014).") libraries have already been developed, also within two of the main robotic middlewares – ROS and YARP – but they require additional contributions from a large community to grow and reach the maturity needed for successful adoption in robotics. Eventually, a hybrid approach that combines frame-based and event-driven modules, and that fosters the growth of the community revolving around it, could favour a more widespread adoption in the robotics domain. However, this hybrid neuromorphic/traditional design strategy would not fully exploit all the advantages of the neuromorphic paradigm.
